coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional fluorescence sensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots and its application for toxoflavin detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Sai
Yang, Ruixi
Wang, Yaping
Gao, Qinqin
Wang, Langhong
Zhou, Guisheng
Sheng, Qinglin
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, food-borne pathogen Pseudomonas cocovenenans have caused many food safety incidents. Therefore, the detection of toxoflavin produced by the bacteria has aroused widespread attention. In this study, two fluorescent sensors were prepared using nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) materials as precursors: (1) Luminol-functionalized NCDs were synthesized by NHS-EDC reaction, and L-NCDs fluorescence sensor was constructed for the detection of toxoflavins in actual food samples. The linear range of the L-NCDs fluorescence sensor is 0.1–50 μM, the detection limit is 0.05 μM and the excitation wavelength is 375 nm. (2) The NCDs-CMC fluorescent film sensor was constructed by incorporating NCDs into sodium carboxymethyl cellulose. It was excited at a wavelength of 385 nm for visual detection of toxoflavin. The film sensor has a linear range of 1–60 μM and a detection limit of 0.45 μM. The results showed that the method has good sensitivity and reliability. This study extends the application of NCDs-based fluorescence sensing to detect toxoflavin. It enables simple, rapid, low-cost sensitive detection and visual monitoring of toxoflavin. Additionally, it offers a fast and convenient new technology for preventing and controlling Pseudomonas cocovenenans contamination in food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Fluorescent sensor
Toxoflavin
Rapid detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of NCDs, L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC film
Fluorescent sensor based on carbon dots for detection of toxoflavin
Fluorescent sensor based on carbon dots for detection of toxoflavin
Selectivity and reproducibility of fluorescent sensors

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Nature Science Foundation of China (82304685), the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (2023-YBSF-245), the Science and Technology Foundation of Xi'an (23NYGG0062), the Scientific Research Program of the Education Department of Shaanxi Provincial Government (23JE035), the Open Project Program of Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Intelligent Food Manufacturing (Foshan University) (GPKLIFM-KF-202304), and 2023 Sanqin Talent Special Support Program Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team Project.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence emission spectra of 0.1 mg/mL luminol, NCDs solution, luminol and NCDs mixed solution and L-NCDs solution under 375 nm excitation; (B) Fluorescence changes of luminol, NCDs and L-NCDs solution after adding 30 μM toxoflavin (Inset: Photo-images of luminol, NCDs and L-NCDs treated with 30 μM concentrations of toxoflavin; (C) Changes of fluorescence emission peaks of NCDs powder, CMC and NCDs-CMC films at 385 nm; (D) Changes of fluorescence emission peaks of HMC, CMCS, PEG, and CMC composite films at 385 nm; Fluorescence excitation emission spectra (E) NCDs, (F) L-NCDs, (G) NCDs-CMC film, and UV–visible absorption spectra of toxoflavin (Interpolated photos of NCDs, L-NCDs, and NCDs-CMC film under the ultraviolet lamp, respectively).

TEM images of (A) NCDs, (B) L-NCDs (interpolated HRTEM images, respectively), and (C) NCDs-CMC; Particle size distribution of (D) NCDs, and (E) L-NCDs; (F) SEM images of NCDs-CMC film surface (interpolated SEM images of NCDs-CMC film section).
(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of NCDs, luminol and L-NCDs and (B) NCDs, CMC and NCDs-CMC. Infrared spectra of (C) NCDs, and (D) L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC.

(A) The L-NCDs based fluorescent sensor in PBS buffer (pH = 8) towards different toxoflavin concentrations (0–120 μM); (B) Standard curve of fluorescence intensity change and added toxoflavin concentration; (C) The NCDs-CMC based fluorescent sensor towards different concentrations of toxoflavin (0–120 μM); (D) The standard curve of fluorescence intensity change (F-F0) and the concentration of toxoflavin (Ct), (E) Photo-images of NCDs-CMC film treated with different concentrations of toxoflavin.
Schematic diagram of toxoflavin detection based on NCDs.
Comparison of toxoflavin detection by different research methods.
Standard addition recovery experiment of fluorescent sensor.
The actual rice flour samples of fluorescence sensor.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional fluorescence sensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots and its application for toxoflavin detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology
Northwest University, Laboratory of Nutritional and Healthy Food-Individuation Manufacturing Engineering/Research Center of Food Safety Risk Assessment and Control

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, food-borne pathogen Pseudomonas cocovenenans have caused many food safety incidents. Therefore, the detection of toxoflavin produced by the bacteria has aroused widespread attention. In this study, two fluorescent sensors were prepared using nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) materials as precursors: (1) Luminol-functionalized NCDs were synthesized by NHS-EDC reaction, and L-NCDs fluorescence sensor was constructed for the detection of toxoflavins in actual food samples. The linear range of the L-NCDs fluorescence sensor is 0.1–50 μM, the detection limit is 0.05 μM and the excitation wavelength is 375 nm. (2) The NCDs-CMC fluorescent film sensor was constructed by incorporating NCDs into sodium carboxymethyl cellulose. It was excited at a wavelength of 385 nm for visual detection of toxoflavin. The film sensor has a linear range of 1–60 μM and a detection limit of 0.45 μM. The results showed that the method has good sensitivity and reliability. This study extends the application of NCDs-based fluorescence sensing to detect toxoflavin. It enables simple, rapid, low-cost sensitive detection and visual monitoring of toxoflavin. Additionally, it offers a fast and convenient new technology for preventing and controlling Pseudomonas cocovenenans contamination in food.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the improvement of people's living standards, people are increasingly paying attention to food safety issues related to human health. Food safety encompasses various aspects, including contamination by food-borne pathogens, mycotoxins, pesticide and veterinary drug residues, as well as the illegal addition of food additives. Among these, food-borne pathogens have attracted global attention due to their extensive impact and high occurrence rates [1]. Toxoflavin is a small molecule fatty acid exotoxin produced by food-borne pathogens such as Pseudomonas cocovenenans, Burkholderia and Pseudomonas glumes [2,3]. It is mainly found in fermented rice noodles, tremella, potatoes and other foods that are easily contaminated by the above pathogens in food safety incidents [4]. Toxoflavin exhibits thermal stability and is resistant to destruction through ordinary cooking methods. Additionally, there is no specific antidote for it, and the mortality rate associated with toxoflavin poisoning can be as high as 40–100 % [5]. Since its first confirmation as a cause in 1979, poisoning incidents resulting from the consumption of various foods contaminated by Pseudomonas cocovenenans have been reported in numerous provinces and cities across China [6,7]. Currently, the primary methods for detecting toxoflavin include spectrophotometry [8,9], thin layer chromatography [10,11], high performance liquid chromatography [12], mass spectrometry [13,14] and biosensor method [15,16]. However, there are limited studies on the detection of toxoflavin using biosensors due to the lack of corresponding aptamers and antibodies. HPLC remains one of the most important detection methods for toxoflavin. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a simple and rapid toxoflavin sensing technology.

Fluorescence sensing has emerged as a rapid and simple method for detection among various detection methods. A fluorescence sensor is a device that utilizes fluorescein or other fluorescent materials to sense targets. These fluorescent materials encompass nano-fluorescent materials (such as carbon dots and perovskites), fluorescent biomolecules, and fluorescein variants like rhodamine and luminol. In a fluorescence sensing system, the fluorescence signal of the fluorescent material changes when the target is identified, such as changes in fluorescence intensity, quantum yield, or lifetime. The target information is converted into an output optical signal to achieve sensing of the target. The working mechanisms of fluorescent sensors include photoelectron transfer (PET) [17–19], resonance energy transfer (RET) [20–22], internal filter effect (IFE) [23], aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) [24], aggregation induced emission (AIE) [25], quencher quenching [26] and other mechanisms. The IFE occurs when the ultraviolet–visible absorption peak of the target being measured aligns with the fluorescence excitation peak of the fluorescence sensing element. In this case, the target and the fluorescent substance compete to absorb the excitation wavelength. As a result, its luminescence is quenched when the available excitation light for the fluorescent substance is reduced. Another common detection method in biosensors involves adding a quencher to the fluorescent material, which reduces the fluorescence intensity. The quenching mechanism can be categorized into two types: fluorescent static quenching (FSQ) and fluorescent dynamic quenching (FDQ). FSQ is caused by the fluorescence quenches during the formation of a complex between the luminescent material with a ground state quencher. FDQ is caused by the collision between the luminescent material and the quenching agent. In this study, the fluorescence sensor operates based on the IFE and FSQ principles.

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with particle sizes ranging from 1 to 20 nm. Xu et al. have unexpectedly discovered excellent optical properties of single-walled carbon nanotubes during their purification process [27]. Yang et al. further classified carbon dots into three types based on their core and surface groups [28]: graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots and carbonized polymer dots. Compared with other carbon nanomaterials like carbon nano-onions, fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, nano-diamonds, graphene and its derivatives, carbon dots combine the optical properties of quantum dots and the physical and chemical properties of carbon nanomaterials, therefore they perform more advantages [29]. CDs have excellent optical properties. In addition, their fluorescence emission can be regulated according to the synthesis [26,30–32]. CDs also have excellent photo-bleaching resistance and luminous efficiency with the fluorescence quantum yield reaching as high as 100 % [33]. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs), a novel type of carbon dot material, involve incorporating nitrogen-containing raw materials into the synthesis process. This doping process introduces a layer of nitrogen atoms on the surface, enhancing the optical properties and electron transfer ability of the carbon dots. The defects caused by nitrogen in nitrogen-doped CDs increase the electronic activity in the conduction band, which can better enhance the luminescence of CDs [34]. Mousazadeh research group have reported various kinds of CDs or NCDs by using citric acid/L-histidine, spirulina algae, m-phenylenediamine/citric acid, spirulina algae/L-arginine, and red beetroot, as different precursors for the synthesized of fluorescence probes [35–39].

In this study, luminol functionalized NCDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method, and L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC fluorescence film were prepared. Based on the fluorescence internal filtration effect and static quenching, L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC fluorescence sensors were constructed, which were used for visual detection of flavonoids and lutein in actual food samples, respectively. Two fluorescence sensors based on carbon dots exhibit high sensitivity, low detection limits, and a wide linear range, and the linear relationship of the method is significant, with good accuracy and precision. The sensors also demonstrate good selectivity and reproducibility in detecting toxoflavin in the presence of other substances commonly found in food. Furthermore, the sensors successfully detect toxoflavin in actual food samples. This research expands the application of fluorescence sensing based on NCDs in the detection of toxoflavin, which realizes the simple, rapid and low-cost sensitive detection and visual monitoring of toxoflavin. It provides a fast and convenient new technology for preventing and controlling Pseudomonas cocovenenans contamination in food.

Citric acid (≥99.5 %), luminol (98 %), toxoflavin (≥98 %), n-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98 %), 1-ethyl-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98 %), lysine (Lys, ≥99 %), α-starch (AR), cystein (Cys, ≥99 %), glycine (Gly, ≥99 %), tyrosine (Tyr, ≥99 %), valine (Val, ≥99 %), tryptophan (Trp, ≥99 %), glucose (Glu), carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS, 543.5 g/mol), hydroxymethyl cellulose (HMC, 4000 mpa.s), chitosan (CMCS), and polyethylene glycol (PEG), Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), Aflatoxin B2 (AFB2), Ochratoxin A (OTA, 98 %), Citrinin (CIT, 98 %) and Zearalenone (ZEN, ≥98 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All the other chemicals used in this study were of analytical reagent grade. Coconut venom pseudomonad Burkholderia gladioli strains were collected from the National Center for Food Safety Risk Assessment. Sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC, 800–1200 mpa.s) was purchased from Xilong Science Co., Ltd. Auricularia auricula and rice flour was obtained from the local market.

The fluorescence spectra were measured by an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer. UV tests were performed on an Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer UV-2600 (Shimadzu, Japan). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were performed using a transmission electron microscope JEM 2800 (Japan Electronics Co., Ltd.). The Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were acquired with a Scanning electron microscope SU8010 (Hitachi, Japan) with a resolution of 8 nm and a magnification of 300,000. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were carried out on the Fourier transform infrared spectrometer IR Affinity-1S (Shimadzu, Japan) with a scanning range of 400–4000 cm−1 and a resolution of 4 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy instrument PHI5000Ver saProbeIII (ULVAC-PHI) with the instrument excitation source Al Kα. The fluorescence lifetime was acquired on the transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer F-4500 (Hitachi, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NCDs, L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC film
ce:section.ce:para: The NCDs were prepared by hydrothermal method [40,41]. After centrifugation, the supernatant was taken and filtered through 0.45 μm and 0.22 μm aqueous microporous membranes, respectively. The product was placed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag and dialyzed at a frequency of changing water every 3h for 36h. The dialysate was freeze-dried in a vacuum freeze-drying machine to obtain a purified dark green carbon dot powder. L-NCDs were synthesized by coupling NCDs with luminol through the cross-linking reaction of EDC and NHS. After the reaction, the carbon dots composite luminol solution was obtained by dialysis with a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 36h. A series of CDs prepared under different ratio of citric acid to urea precursors, and the ratios of carbon dots to luminol was shown in Fig. S1 of the Supporting information.

The NCDs-CMC film was prepared by adding 0.1 g of the above carbon dots and 0.3 g CMC into 5 mL ultrapure water and stirred. The mixture was added to a 15 mL beaker and mechanically stirred at 90 °C for 4 h [29] NCDs-CMC was stored at 4 °C in the dark. The viscous product is evenly applied to a Φ10 mm * 1 mm quartz sheet. After drying, the NCDs-CMC films were obtained.

The relative fluorescence QY is a key parameter used to evaluate the luminescence efficiency of fluorescent materials. In the current work, the QY was determined with reference to quinine sulfate in 0.05 M sulfuric acid solution as the standard (QY of 0.54 [42]). The following equation was employed to calculate the fluorescence QY of the NCDs, L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC samples:
(1)
QY =
F u
/
F r
×
A r
/
A u
×
η u
/
η r
where QY is the fluorescence quantum yield; Fu and Fr are the integrated areas of the fluorescence emission spectra of the sample and the standard, respectively; Au and Ar are the absorbances of the sample and the standard, respectively; ηu and ηr are the refractive indices of the solvents used for the sample and the standard, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensor based on carbon dots for detection of toxoflavin
ce:section.ce:para: The aqueous solution of 0.01–120 μm toxoflavin was prepared and was added to the cuvette, then 200 μL 0.1 M PBS solution (pH = 8) and 200 μL L-NCDs (1 mg/mL) were added to the final volume of 2 mL, and then fluorescence test was carried out at 375 nm. 90 μL of 0.1–120 μm of toxoflavin aqueous solution was added to 10 μL of 0.1 M PBS solution (pH = 8). After mixing, 7 μL was evenly dropped onto the surface of NCDs-CMC and dried. The fluorescence emission test was carried out at 385 nm. Each sample and blank were determined three times. The NCDs-CMC film quartz sheets coated with 0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 μm toxoflavin were placed under a 365 nm dark box ultraviolet analyzer to take their fluorescence photographs.

Black fungus and rice flour were selected as actual samples for the next test. 10 g dried Auricularia auricula was soaked in 100 mL ultrapure water, 100 g rice flour was added to 500 mL water and boiled. The standard addition method was used to add different standard concentrations of toxoflavin solution (concentration gradient of 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 30 μm), in which the concentration of toxoflavin in the standard addition method was referred to the concentration range of toxoflavin in the actual sample. Then, 200 μL 0.1 M PBS solution (pH = 8) and 200 μL L-NCDs (1 mg/mL) were added to the final volume of 2 mL. After mixing, the fluorescence test was carried out at 375 nm. 90 μL of the above mixture was added to 10 μL PBS solution, and 7 μL was evenly dropped onto the NCDs-CMC film. Each sample was measured three times, and the fluorescence emission peak was recorded.

The toxicity test of actual food samples was carried out on the two strains of Burkholderia gladioli. After activation, 1 mL of Burkholderia gladioli strains was inoculated into 25 g rice flour samples in an ultra-clean bench. The rice flour samples were divided into coated and non-coated groups. The rice flour inoculated with Pseudomonas cocovenenans was placed in a constant temperature incubator at 26 °C for 3 days. Then the NCDs-CMC material was coated on the inoculated rice flour and dried. The fluorescence quenching was observed and photographed under the ultraviolet lamp and the fluorescence test was performed at 375 nm. All measurements were repeated in triplicate independently and presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD).

To evaluate the practical application capability of the present system, HPLC (LC-2030, Shimadzu, Janpan) with a reverse-phase C18 (5 µm) stainless steel column (4.6 × 250 mm) was used to determine the results [43]. Briefly, methanol–water aqueous solution (0.005 % acetic acid) was used as the mobile phase, the detection wavelength was 258 nm, and the flow rate was 0.8 mL/min. All sample extracts were filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane before HPLC detection. Concentration of toxoflavin was determined by comparing its peak area with that of a toxoflavin standard.

The NCDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid and urea at 180 °C for 4h. The L-NCDs fluorescent sensor was prepared by coupling with luminol through NHS-EDC reaction, as shown in Scheme 1 . The nitrogen-doped carbon dots synthesized by this method have a large number of amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups on the surface, which emit blue fluorescence. Since the optimal fluorescence excitation peak of L-NCDs overlaps with the UV–visible absorption peak of toxoflavin, the excitation light of L-NCDs is absorbed by the fluorescence inner filter effect (IFE) to quench its fluorescence emission. Luminol also interacted with the group of toxoflavin to form new substances when toxoflavin was added, which reduced the energy released by the luminescent group in luminol from the excited state to the ground state, and reduced the fluorescence of L-NCDs by static quenching (FSQ). Therefore, a fluorescence sensor for the detection of toxoflavin can be established by the change in fluorescence signal intensity of L-NCDs.

The dark green NCDs-CMC material was prepared by mixing the prepared NCDs with CMC and stirring at 90 °C for 4h. The NCDs-CMC fluorescent film was prepared by coating it on a quartz sheet and drying at 50 °C for 30 min. Due to the strong hydrogen bond interaction between CMC and NCDs, the NCDs fluorophore is protected from quenching in the solid state. Due to the overlap between the fluorescence excitation peak of NCDs-CMC and the ultraviolet absorption peak of toxoflavin, the fluorescence emission of NCDs-CMC is reduced by the fluorescence IFE. The fluorescence quenching of the film could be observed under ultraviolet light. Based on this, a visual fluorescent film detection of toxoflavin by NCDs-CMC was established.

The fluorescence emission spectra of luminol, NCDs, a simple mixture of luminol and NCDs, and L-NCDs were measured, as shown in Fig. 1 A. It can be observed that the fluorescence of the two is significantly reduced after simple mixing, but the fluorescence emission of the two was better after coupling. The fluorescence quenching may be a result of the aggregation of the surface groups of the two components after a simple mix. The composites are then cross-linked by NHS-EDC to prevent direct interaction of the surface groups. Additionally, the connection of carbon dots and luminol lead to enhance fluorescence, possibly due to the promotion of photoelectron transfer by luminol. The fluorescence emission quenching of luminol, NCDs, and L-NCDs with 30 μm toxinflavin were compared (Fig. 1B). The fluorescence quenching of luminol to toxoflavin may be attributed to the reaction between luminol and the surface group of toxoflavin [4]. On the other hand, the quenching of NCDs to toxoflavin is caused by the fluorescence inner filter effect. It is evident that L-NCDs not only exhibit the highest fluorescence performance at the same concentration, but also demonstrate the largest fluorescence quenching, thereby enhancing the sensitivity of toxoflavin detection.

Similarly, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of NCDs-CMC were tested by fluorescence spectrophotometer, as shown in Fig. 1C. The good fluorescence of NCDs-CMC film can also be observed under an ultraviolet lamp. When fluorescence detection was performed on NCDs solid powder or CMC film alone, no fluorescence emission was observed, while NCDs-CMC film showed good fluorescence emission at 385 nm. At the same time, NCDs were embedded in HMC, CMCS, and PEG matrix, respectively. It can be seen from Fig. 1D that only strong fluorescence emission could be generated when NCDs were embedded in the CMC films. This is because a strong hydrogen bond interaction is established between cellulose and NCDs, which protects the fluorophore from quenching [44].

L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC were selected as the materials of the fluorescence sensor due to the superior fluorescence properties of the fluorescent probe. For the subsequent preparation of NCDs-CMC, NCDs with a raw material ratio of citric acid and urea of 1:5 were chosen, as shown in Fig. 1D. As shown in Fig. 1E, it demonstrated the excitation and emission spectra of L-NCDs synthesized by luminol and NCDs at a mass ratio of 1:5, as well as the UV–Vis absorption spectra of toxinflavins. The optimal excitation wavelength is 375 nm, and the optimal emission is about 437 nm. The change in fluorescence excitation and emission peak positions of L-NCDs compared to NCDs is attributed to the addition of luminol. The optimal excitation wavelength is 385 nm, and the optimal emission is about 451 nm. The excitation spectrum peak of NCDs-CMC exhibited a red shift compared to NCDs (Fig. 1F), which could be attributed to the incorporation of sodium carboxymethyl cellulose polymer. This incorporation enhances the material's conjugate force, resulting in a red shift in the excitation spectrum [45]. The red shift of the excitation spectrum makes the UV absorption peak of NCDs-CMC closer to the UV absorption peak of toxinflavin, which improves the sensitivity of the carbon dot material to detect toxoflavin. Since the fluorescence emission peaks of L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC at 375 and 385 nm overlapped with the UV absorption peak of toxoflavin at 393 nm, the fluorescence emission quenching of L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC after the addition of toxoflavin was partly attributed to the fluorescence internal filtering effect. Meanwhile, the fluorescence QYs of L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC under 375 and 385 nm excitation were calculated to be 27.8 % and 19.3 % through fluorescence spectra and UV visible absorption spectra.

The NCDs and L-NCDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy and the particle size distribution was drawn. As shown in Fig. 2 A and D, the prepared NCDs were uniformly dispersed without aggregation, and the particle size distribution was narrow. In the range of 1–3 nm, the average particle size was about 2.12 nm. The TEM characterization and particle size distribution of L-NCDs were described in Fig. 2B and E. The particle size ranged from 2 to 5 nm, with an average size of approximately 3.21 nm. The HRTEM images proved that both NCDs and L-NCDs exhibited an amorphous carbon structure. This suggests that the luminescence mechanism of the NCDs synthesized in this paper is primarily attributed to the surface state, specifically the presence of abundant surface functional groups on the carbon dots. Additionally, the introduction of nitrogen elements resulted in enhanced luminescence performance through the induction of defects. The NCDs-CMC films were characterized by TEM and SEM (Fig. 2C and F). Fig. 2F showed the surface of the NCDs-CMC film under the SEM lens, and the thickness of the film was about 106.8 μm.

UV–vis tests were performed on NCDs, luminol, L-NCDs and NCDs, CMC, and NCDs-CMC, respectively. The results showed that the UV–visible absorption peaks of NCDs were mainly at 233 nm and 331 nm, corresponding to π–π* electron transition and n–π* transition, respectively, indicating the presence of sp2 hybrid carbon atoms and CO/CON. The UV–visible absorption peaks of L-NCDs (Fig. 3 A) and NCDs-CMC (Fig. 3B) are not a simple addition of the precursors, but the absorption peaks have changed. The absorption of L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC at 331 nm is enhanced, indicating the generation of amide and hydrogen bonds. The above results show that the preparation of L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC is successful. NCDs, L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC composites were characterized by FTIR. As shown in Fig. 3C, the peaks of NCDs at 3184, 3012, 2841, 2358 and 1558 cm−1 are the stretching vibrations of OH, NH, CH, CO and CN, respectively. The peaks at 1396, 983 and 821 cm−1 are the stretching vibrations of CO, COC and COH, respectively. The results confirmed the synthesis of NCDs with carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface. As shown in Fig. 3D, compared with NCDs, L-NCDs have three new peaks at 3662, 2989 and 1629 cm−1, which are the NH stretching vibration of the primary amide, the CH stretching vibration on the unsaturated carbon, and the CO stretching vibration of the primary amide bond in the primary amide band. The NH and CO stretching vibrations of the primary amides are generated by the amide bond formed by the combination of luminol and NCDs, which further confirms the successful combination of carbon dots and luminol. The infrared spectrum of NCDs-CMC exhibits three increased absorption peaks at 3668, 2978 and 1053 cm−1 compared to NCDs. These peaks correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH, CH, and COC, respectively. This indicates the successful embedding of CMC in NCDs and the formation of numerous hydrogen bonds, confirming the successful combination of NCDs and CMC. The elements and groups of NCDs, L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC were tested by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy as shown in Figs. S2–S4 in the Supporting information. All the above results suggest the successful synthesis of NCDs, L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC composites.

To explore the quenching mechanism of NCDs’ determination of toxoflavin, the optical properties of NCDs and toxoflavin were recorded. Since the toxoflavin itself does not emit light and the UV absorption does not overlap with the emission wavelength of the L-NCDs, there is no fluorescence resonance energy transfer. The lowest unoccupied orbital energy level of L-NCDs is lower than that of toxoflavin, so the excited state electrons of L-NCDs cannot be transferred from the lowest unoccupied orbital of L-NCDs to toxoflavin, and there is no photoinduced electron transfer. There was no aggregation of L-NCDs after the addition of toxoflavin. Since the UV absorption of toxoflavin overlaps with the excitation spectra of L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC, the fluorescence quenching of L-NCDs is dominated by the fluorescence inner filter effect.

The fluorescence lifetime of L-NCDs with and without toxoflavin was tested and compared with the fluorescence lifetime of NCDs and luminol, as shown in Fig. S5A of the supporting information. The fluorescence decay curves could be best fitted with a biexponential function (2):
(2)
R
t
=
B 1
exp
( - t /
τ 1
)
+
B 2
exp
( - t /
τ 2
)
where R(t) is the intensity usually assumed to decay as the sum of individual single exponential decays, B 1 and B 2 are the pre-exponential factors, τ1 and τ2 are the decay times. The average life time τ ave of NCDs upon addition of different concentration of toxinflavin was determined by Eq. (3):
(3)
τ
ave
=
(
B 1
τ 1 2
+
B 2
τ 2 2
)
/
(
B 1
τ 1
+
B 2
τ 2
)

The lifetime results are listed in Table S1. After fitting the fluorescence lifetime, the fluorescence lifetime of L-NCDs at the same concentration was 5.45 ns, slightly lower than the fluorescence lifetime of carbon dots (6.93 ns) and higher than the fluorescence lifetime of luminol (2.62 ns). Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime of the composite material may depend on luminol and carbon dots and their ratio. The results demonstrated that the fluorescence lifetime of L-NCDs remained unchanged at 5.15 ns after the addition of toxoflavin. This suggests that the quenching was likely due to either the internal filter effect or static quenching. The UV spectra of L-NCDs with and without toxoflavin were measured and presented in Fig. S5B. The addition of toxoflavin to the L-NCDs solution resulting in a shift in the UV absorption peak of L-NCDs from 336 nm to 350 nm. This shift suggests that the two substances reacted and static quenching took place, which was in line with the findings of the fluorescence lifetime analysis.

The fluorescence lifetime of NCDs-CMC with and without toxoflavin was measured and compared with NCDs. As shown in Fig. S5C, the fluorescence lifetime of NCDs-CMC was 13.90 ns, which was higher than that of NCDs. This is attributed to the strong hydrogen bonding interaction between CMCs and NCDs that protects fluorophores from quenching and therefore increases fluorescence lifetime. The results showed that there was no significant difference in the fluorescence lifetime of NCDs-CMC after the addition of toxinflavin at 13.6 ns, which proved that the quenching was caused by the internal filtration effect or static quenching. The ultraviolet spectrum of NCDs-CMC with and without toxinflavin was measured, as shown in Fig. S5D. It was found that the positions of absorption peaks of the two films did not change when toxinflavin was added to the surface of NCDs-CMC films. This indicates that only the fluorescence internal filtering effect occurs.

The influence of ionic strength on the fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs was also investigated. Results showed that the fluorescence spectra were almost constant under an aqueous NaCl solution (0–2.5 M), even when the concentration of NaCl was as high as 2.5 M. This result suggested that the NCDs have excellent optical stability and have great potential for sensing applications under physiological conditions [36,46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensor based on carbon dots for detection of toxoflavin
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized conditions, L-NCDs were used to detect a series of concentrations of toxoflavin (0.01–120 μm) (Fig. 4 A). The fluorescence intensity of L-NCDs decreased with the increase of toxoflavin concentration. It was found that there was a significant linear relationship between ΔF and Ct in the range of 0.1–50 μm (Fig. 4B). The linear equation was ΔF = 50.17 Ct − 35.24 (R = 0.9984), and the detection limit (LOD, 3σ/k) was 0.05 μm according to the standard deviation of 11 blanks. The conversion of the standard limit of 0.1 μg/mL of toxoflavin in the group standard was about 0.518 μm. At the same time, we carried out the F test and t test for the standard curve of toxoflavin. The results show that the designed fluorescence sensing platform has good detection performance such as high sensitivity, low detection limit and wide linear range, and the linear relationship of the method is significant, with good accuracy and precision.

Under the optimized conditions, the aqueous solution of 0.1–120 μm toxoflavin was prepared and uniformly dropped onto the NCDs-CMC film, and the fluorescence test was performed under the excitation of 385 nm. As shown in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs-CMC decreased with the increase of toxoflavin concentration. There is a clear linear relationship between ΔF and Ct (Fig. 4D), the linear equation is ΔF = 40.01 Ct + 9.37 (R = 0.9975), the linear range is 0.7–60 μm, and the LOD obtained by the standard deviation of 11 blanks is 0.45 μm. The fluorescence of NCDs-CMC was observed under a 365 nm UV lamp, as shown in Fig. 4E. When the concentration of toxoflavin increased from 1 μm to 60 μm, the fluorescence of NCDs-CMC was visibly quenched. Since the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots in a solid membrane state is slightly lower than that in an aqueous solution, and the absence of the static quenching effect of lutein, the detection limit of this film is not as good as that of L-NCDs. Compared with L-NCDs, NCDs-CMC film is more simple and convenient for the detection of toxoflavin, and can be observed directly with the naked eye to achieve visual detection. The results show that the designed thin film fluorescence sensor has good detection performance such as high sensitivity, low detection limit and wide linear range, and the linear relationship of the method is significant, with good accuracy and precision. Due to the lack of corresponding aptamers and antibodies, there have been few biosensors for the detection of toxoflavin in recent years. Compared with Table 1 , the fluorescence sensor in this study is low-cost, simple, and convenient, with a wide linear range and low detection limit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and reproducibility of fluorescent sensors
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the selectivity of the fluorescent sensor, interfering substances were added to the PBS buffer to test the fluorescence of the fluorescent sensor. 0.1 M Ca2+, 0.1 M Fe2+, 0.1 mM Fe3+, 0.01 M vitamin B1, 0.01 M vitamin B2, 0.1 M Lys, 0.1 M Cys, 0.1 M Gly, 0.1 M Tyr, 0.1 M Val, 0.1 M Trp, 0.1 M Glu, 0.5 g/mL α-starch, 0.3 mM 9. 9,10-Phenanthraquinone, 0.3 mM juglone, 0.3 mM BKA, 30 μm AFB1, 30 μm AFB2, 0.3 mM OTA, 0.3 mM CIT and 0.3 mM ZEN were measured the fluorescence intensity (Fig. S6). The selection of ions, vitamins, amino acids, glucose and starch is attributed to their possible coexistence in foods susceptible to toxoflavin infection, such as fungi and rice noodles. Due to their structural similarity to toxoflavin, 9,10-phenanthraquinone and walnuts are chosen. Bongkrekic acid and other toxins are easy to coexist with toxoflavin. The results showed that the fluorescence reduction of L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC could be ignored in the presence of other interfering substances. The content of VB1 and VB2 in the actual samples such as agaric and rice flour is about 1/10 of 0.01 M, and the relative standard deviation is calculated according to the actual sample content standard. It was found that Fe3+ ions could induce quenching of the L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC fluorescence responses under the concentration of 0.1 mM. Such quenching process can be ascribed to the formation of a chelating complex between Fe3+ ions and the surface functional groups of NCDs or the transfer of energy from the surface groups of the NCDs to the d-orbital of the Fe atoms [50]. However, the influence of Fe3+ ions is very limited, since the Fe3+ can not reach to such a high content within the actual samples. Five identical L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC were prepared by the same method and their reproducibility was verified. The average relative standard deviations (RSD) of the two materials prepared for the same concentration of toxoflavin were 0.47 % and 0.36 %, respectively. The maximum deviations of the fluorescence response values during the five preparation processes were 0.31 % and 0.27 %, respectively, which indicates that L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC had good reproducibility. The stability of the fluorescence sensor based on L-NCDs is shown in Fig. S7 of the Supporting information. Figs. S8–S10 of the Supporting information shows the fluorescence stability and the effect of temperature on the fluorescence of L-NCDs.

Black fungus and rice flour were selected as actual samples for further testing. The water of Auricularia auricular and the soup of boiled rice flour were used to analyze the toxoflavin in the actual sample by the standard addition method. Table 2 shows that the detection results of the two fluorescent sensors for toxoflavin are basically the same, and there is no significant difference between the two data groups by one-way analysis of variance. The recovery of toxoflavin by L-NCDs sensor was between 90.2 % and 118.3 %, and the relative standard deviation was between 0.23 % and 0.31 %. The recoveries of NCDs-CMC for toxoflavin were in the range of 92.7–107.7 %, and the relative standard deviations were in the range of 0.20–0.26 %. This shows that the sensor has a high recovery rate and good accuracy, which can be applied to detect toxic flavin in actual samples.

Toxoflavin was detected in the actual rice flour samples inoculated with Strain 1 and Strain 2, and toxoflavin was detected in the rice flour samples inoculated with the two strains cultured for 12 h, 24 h, 48 h and 72 h as shown in Table 3 . The toxicity of the Strain 1 group was slightly higher than that of the Strain 2 group at the same time, and the relative standard deviation of L-NCDs was 0.25–0.3 %. The relative standard deviation of NCDs-CMC was about 0.20–0.26 %. The results showed that the L-NCDs and NCDs CMC detection results of the same strain cultured at the same time were basically consistent, and there was no significant difference between the two groups of data by one-way analysis of variance. Furthermore, there was no significant difference between the results obtained by the sensors and an HPLC method.

The photo of NCDs-CMC coated rice noodles sample Strain 1 group and Strain 2 group under ultraviolet light at 0 h, 12 h, 24 h, 48 h, and 72 h during the toxin generation process (Fig. S11). The concentration of toxin production measured in the above table is compared with the fluorescence film photo with a standard concentration of toxoflavin. The results are basically consistent, which proves that visual sensors can be applied to the detection of actual samples. This work also provides a fast and convenient new technology for the prevention and control of Pseudomonas cocovenenans contamination in food and product contamination monitoring.

In summary, luminol functionalized L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC fluorescent films were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis of NCDs materials. The fluorescence quantum yields were 27.8 % and 19.3 %, and the fluorescence lifetimes were 5.45 ns and 13.90 ns, respectively. L-NCDs and NCDs-CMC fluorescent sensors were constructed for the detection of toxoflavin based on fluorescence internal filtering effect and static quenching. The stability, selectivity and reproducibility of the fluorescent sensors were investigated, and finally applied to detect toxoflavin in actual samples. The linear ranges of the two fluorescent sensors were 0.1–50 μm and 1–60 μm, and the detection limits were 0.05 μm and 0.45 μm, respectively. The fluorescence sensor designed in this paper has excellent detection performance including high sensitivity, low detection limit and wide linear range. It also exhibited good accuracy, precision, selectivity, and reproducibility. Moreover, this research lays the foundation for visual real-time monitoring and detecting changes in the content of food toxic flavin.

Sai Zhang: Writing – original draft, Supervision. Ruixi Yang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Yaping Wang: Software, Methodology. Qinqin Gao: Formal analysis, Data curation. Langhong Wang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Guisheng Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Qinglin Sheng: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Nature Science Foundation of China (82304685), the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (2023-YBSF-245), the Science and Technology Foundation of Xi'an (23NYGG0062), the Scientific Research Program of the Education Department of Shaanxi Provincial Government (23JE035), the Open Project Program of Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Intelligent Food Manufacturing (Foshan University) (GPKLIFM-KF-202304), and 2023 Sanqin Talent Special Support Program Innovation and Entrepreneurship Team Project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112168.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
